risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Infants were randomized to receive one of two liquid supplements to be taken daily until they reached 18 months of age. Randomization was in blocks of two stratified by infant gender." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants were randomized and gives contextual detail that the sequence used blocked and stratified randomization, which supports a proper random allocation process for sequence generation.

Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable. A set of bottles were coded with each subject’s unique study identification number to facilitate distribution. The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism in which identical coded supplement bottles were used and the allocation list was kept away from those enrolling/distributing participants, preventing recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable." and "The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study." Comment: These statements indicate that the two liquid supplements were made to look the same and that treatment codes were concealed until study end, supporting successful blinding of participants/caregivers and study personnel distributing the intervention. Because the intervention was masked and outcomes included largely objective biochemical measures, performance bias is unlikely to have materially affected the study overall.

Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable... The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study." Quote: "Hemoglobin and hematocrit were assessed at the clinic using standard methods by study nurse... Plasma ferritin was determined at the IIN laboratory by ELISA... Plasma copper and zinc and urinary zinc concentrations were determined at the IIN laboratory by atomic absorption spectrophotometry." Comment: Outcomes in this report were assessed mainly by study nurse/laboratory systems. The study indicates masking of allocation through indistinguishable supplements and concealed allocation list until study end, and the reported outcomes here are objective biochemical measures assessed by standard lab methods/automated assays, which are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "As shown in the study profile (Figure 1), 275 infants were evaluated for eligibility, and 251 were randomized, 122 to the iron + copper group, and 129 to the iron + copper + zinc group. As shown, there were 42 (13%) infants who left the study, leaving 108 in the iron + copper group and 101 in the iron + copper + zinc group." and "The enrollment characteristics of those who completed the study were compared with those lost to follow up... those lost to follow up were significantly more likely to be girls and to be allocated to the supplemental zinc group. As shown in Figure 1, there were more children who left the study due to lack of time or because they disliked the supplement in the zinc group." Comment: Post-randomization attrition is reported: 14/122 (11.5%) in iron+cuprer vs 28/129 (21.7%) in iron+cuprer+zinc, a differential loss of about 10.2 percentage points. Reasons were also imbalanced, with more withdrawals in the zinc group because they disliked the supplement. This meets the threshold for high risk because the paper reports per-arm losses establishing differential attrition that could bias the comparison.
